examples.title: System Call Transition
examples.description: Demonstrates how a system call causes mode switch from User to Kernel Mode
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the flow when a user process makes a system call. The TRAP instruction causes the CPU to switch from User Mode to Kernel Mode. The kernel validates and executes the request, then returns control to user mode.

examples.title: System Call Example in C
examples.description: Demonstrates a read system call that switches from User to Kernel Mode
examples.explanation: The read() function is a C library wrapper around a system call. When executed, it triggers a software interrupt (trap) that switches the CPU to Kernel Mode. The kernel performs the actual file reading using privileged I/O instructions, then returns to User Mode.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is bytes to read

examples.title: User Mode to Kernel Mode via OS Module
examples.description: Demonstrates system calls in Python that require Kernel Mode privileges
examples.code:
import os

def demonstrate_kernel_mode_access():
    # These operations require SYSTEM CALLS to kernel
    
    # System Call 1: Open file (requires kernel to access filesystem)
    fd = os.open("example.txt", os.O_RDONLY)
    # At this point, CPU switched from User → Kernel → User
    
    # System Call 2: Read from file descriptor
    data = os.read(fd, 1024)
    # Another mode switch: User → Kernel → User
    
    # System Call 3: Get process information
    pid = os.getpid()  # Calls kernel to get process info
    
    # System Call 4: Create new process (fork)
    child_pid = os.fork()  # Complex: creates process in kernel
    
    # Each os module function triggers system call
    # CPU switches modes behind the scenes
    
    os.close(fd)
    return data

# Running in USER MODE
# All the above operations cause implicit mode switches
# The programmer doesn't see the mode transitions
# But each system call involves: User → Kernel → User
examples.explanation: Python's os module functions are wrappers around system calls. Each function like os.open(), os.read(), os.getpid(), or os.fork() triggers a transition to Kernel Mode where the actual privileged operation is performed. The programmer works in User Mode while the OS handles kernel-level operations.
examples.timeComplexity: Varies by operation (O(n) for read, O(1) for getpid)

examples.title: Privileged Instruction Check
examples.description: Demonstrates how hardware prevents privileged instruction execution in User Mode
examples.explanation: This algorithm shows how CPU hardware enforces privilege levels. Before executing any privileged instruction, the hardware checks the Current Privilege Level (CPL) against the Required Privilege Level (RPL). If the CPL is higher (less privileged) than required, a protection fault is generated, causing a trap to Kernel Mode.
